In common law, jurisdiction over something is confined to that which it is actually in possession of. For example, if a tenant moves into your property and now claims title to the land, you have jurisdiction over that land only if you are the tenant. If the tenant then moves out, your new title does not change your possession of the land, and you should file an action to reclaim possession. Civil law, on the other hand, applies to all occurrences in which there is no actual legal possession or control of something. In this sense, a contract or act of Congress nullifies any legal possession or control of something.
